title: Beijing’s Campaign Against Overcapacity Creates Private Credit Opportunities
dek: Tackling overcapacity has moved to the top of Beijing's list of priorities. We're likely witnessing the start of a yearslong transition that will require creative financing solutions – and create new opportunities for private credit, particularly in the area of asset restructurings.

paragraphs: ShoreVest’s private credit strategy comes down to three interrelated sub-strategies: (1) asset backed lending, (2) asset restructuring and (3) debt resolutions or investing in distressed debt, such as non-performing loans (NPLs). From a macro perspective, when the economy is deleveraging, in transition, or trying to stabilize, such opportunities appear in significant volume.

Where the economy is in transition from old economic drivers to new more advanced drivers, it often produces asset restructuring opportunities because bad balance sheets need to be cleaned up, and good assets need to be repurposed. For instance, in early 2015, ShoreVest bought a portfolio of NPLs in Shanghai, many of which were first-lien loans collateralized by warehouses owned by defunct steel industry borrowers. To monetize these loans and collateral, ShoreVest found new economically viable homes for these warehouses with buyers in the e-commerce industry who were short on logistics assets.

One of the biggest economic challenges facing China at the moment, and one which will require significant transition, is overcapacity. Not only is it responsible for entrenched producer price deflation, but it's also eroded corporate profits and resulted in falling tax revenue for the government.

Consequently, in recent months, tackling overcapacity has moved to the top of Beijing's list of priorities. Efforts have only just begun in earnest, and key questions – such as who should bear the cost of factory closures, how quickly should excess capacity be shuttered, and how assets might be repurposed – remain unanswered. That said, we're likely witnessing the start of a yearslong transition that will require creative financing solutions – and create new opportunities for private credit, particularly in the area of asset restructurings.

"Overcapacity" refers to when an industry is capable of producing far more goods than the market demands. It's not a technical term – identifying when an industry is suffering from overcapacity is more art than science. Still, it's fair to say that a broad swathe of Chinese industries are in a state of overcapacity. Those industries can broadly be divided into two categories: sunset and sunrise.

Sunset industries are typically those whose fortunes were linked to the housing sector, like steel, flat glass, cement, home appliances, and construction machinery. For these industries, demand will never again be as strong as it was prior to the housing market's peak in 2021. Many in these industries have ramped up exports to make up for the collapse in domestic demand, but they've done so by cutting prices, thereby exacerbating trade tensions.

Sunrise industries are innovative sectors that Beijing is trying to encourage, like electric vehicles, batteries, and solar panels. Overcapacity has emerged in these industries for two main reasons. The first is that local governments have blindly encouraged the development of these industries, helping keep alive firms that would otherwise have collapsed without support. The second is that firms in these sectors are striving to maximize market share rather than profit in the hope that they can drive their competitors out of business. But with local governments helping keep underperforming firms alive, that process is taking far longer than it should.

Overcapacity is evident in other industries as well – like coal and petrochemicals – largely because of overinvestment encouraged by local authorities. But it's most concentrated in sunrise and sunset industries.

Overcapacity periodically afflicts China's economy. The last state crackdown lasted from 2015 to 2019. This time, Beijing identified overcapacity as a concern in December 2023 when the Central Economic Work Conference – an annual meeting of senior economic policymakers – labelled overcapacity "in some industries" as a major challenge for the year ahead.

However, authorities initially did little to tackle the problem. Instead, they left it to market participants to deal with the problem themselves. However, problems persisted and even worsened. As of August, producer prices had declined for three consecutive years.

The first sign that Beijing intended to take a more hands-on approach to dealing with overcapacity came on June 30 when the People's Daily ran a front-page editorial that blamed local governments for the emergence of overcapacity in key cleantech industries. It said:
bullets: “Misaligned local priorities have exacerbated the [overcapacity] situation. Many local governments have blindly pursued capacity expansion and duplicative projects – engaging in subsidy arms races and offering excessive preferential policies that have severely distorted resource allocation.”
“Improper intervention by local governments in distressed firms has stalled the exit of inefficient and obsolete capacity, further exacerbating vicious market competition.”

paragraphs: The following day, Qiushi – the Party's top journal – waded in, arguing that:
bullets: “Local government overreach…driven by flawed performance metrics that prioritize short-term growth…has fueled local protectionism, market fragmentation, and triggered a race to the bottom in policy incentives.”

“Shortcomings in [China’s] bankruptcy and restructuring regime – combined with interference from local governments – have prevented the timely exit of obsolete and excess capacity, leading to a rise in zombie firms that’s holding back industrial consolidation.”

paragraphs: Then on July 2, the Central Commission for Financial and Economic Affairs (CFFEA) – the Party's top policymaking body for economic issues – said it wanted the government to rein in the price wars being waged in many industries, and support the "orderly exit of outdated capacity."
Speaking at the CFFEA meeting, Xi Jinping made it clear that local officials would be held responsible for creating overcapacity, saying the Party would:
bullets: “Promptly correct behaviors such as illegal investment-promotion [and] local protectionism"

paragraphs: Beijing's efforts are already having an effect. In August, fixed asset investment dropped sharply across industries where Beijing is targeting overcapacity. Investment in:
bullets: Metal smelting and pressing facilities fell 16.7% y/y
Electrical equipment manufacturing – which includes solar panels and batteries – dropped 9.5%

paragraphs: However, while the decline in investment will limit the problem from worsening, closing down existing production facilities is likely to prove far more difficult.

The biggest challenge will be deciding who bears the cost of closing excess capacity. The burden could potentially be borne by local governments, shareholders, creditors, workers, or some combination of all of the above. Or, as is the case for the polysilicon sector, the costs look set to fall upon industry leaders.

Polysilicon is the raw material used in solar components, and the industry's efforts to deal with overcapacity are more advanced than those of any other sector. Since May, China's biggest polysilicon firms have been in talks to jointly establish a RMB 50 billion fund that would acquire outdated production facilities from small domestic firms, and shut them down. The goal is to reduce the industry's annual production capacity from 3-3.2 million metric tons to approximately 2 million tons. That's still well above projected 2025 market demand of 1.4–1.6 million tons, but it would be enough to boost prices, which have collapsed in recent years.
The challenge for the participants – who are mostly private sector firms – is agreeing on how much each company should contribute to the fund, and what the fund should pay for outdated plants.
It was originally hoped an agreement would be reached in Q3 this year. However, in testament to the difficulties of the negotiations, an agreement no longer seems likely before 2026.

It's also unclear how quickly Beijing wants to reduce capacity. The issue is unquestionably urgent. Without an end to overcapacity, deflation will persist, dampening domestic demand. However, a rapid adjustment could be traumatic for the economy, particularly if it results in layoffs, bankruptcies, and further financial stress of local governments.

Petrochemical giant Rongsheng Petrochemical recently disclosed that it expects Beijing to close 10% of its industry's installed capacity, and that the process will take three-to-five years. That suggests Beijing hopes to limit any disruption to the economy by drawing out the process over a relatively long period of time. It's also worth noting that the last campaign to rein in overcapacity in 2015 also lasted about five years.

What's clear is that the process will require M&A, corporate restructuring, and the disposal of distressed assets. While the banks will undoubtedly play a role, this sort of activity falls outside of their typical operations, and often requires an experienced credit solution provider who can identify which collateral assets might be repurposed to better forward-looking uses. We've written previously about how overinvestment in renewable energy and cleantech will create opportunities for private credit providers like ShoreVest. However, as Beijing pursues an even broader campaign to rein in overcapacity throughout the economy, it appears the opportunities may extend well beyond green industries.
